guest_role: Incoming Venture Partner, First Round Capital (former Dropbox VP of Product & Design); also co-guest Finn Barnes, Partner at First Round Capital
assertiveness_why: Harry is largely celebratory and admiring given the exclusive news announcement, asking mostly open, flattering questions, with only one notable moment where he directly contests conventional CAC/LTV thinking and asks the guests to respond.
question_style: Predominantly open-ended, warmly framed questions built around celebrating the news and drawing out career narrative; Harry occasionally injects his own contrarian view (e.g., on CAC/LTV at pre-seed) and asks guests to react, but rarely interrupts or contests answers directly, and lets long uninterrupted monologues run.
tone: Matey, effusive and fan-like throughout, with self-deprecating asides (Instagram plug, 'flunking the schedule', joking about his own hair loss during the ad read) and genuine enthusiasm for the guests' accomplishments.
depth: Mostly narrative/strategic rather than technical — covers product culture, business model transition (consumer to SaaS), product-market-fit frameworks, and venture firm succession/partnership dynamics, with light technical grounding in metrics like LTV:CAC ratios.
harry_quotes: What advice would you give to Todd sitting around this fire?
What does it take to be a great coach to founders, do you think?
If the time came again, would you do it again?
learnings: Picking the right market is arguably the single most important early decision a founder makes, even ahead of the product itself.
Company culture tends to be a direct extension of the founders' personalities and values, and successful firms work to codify and communicate that culture deliberately as they scale.
CAC-to-LTV ratios are largely unknowable and low-value as a diligence criterion at pre-seed/seed stage; what matters is whether founders understand these metrics will matter later.
Effective investor-coaches resist the instinct to hand founders answers and instead help founders work through problems themselves.
Generational partner transitions are a leading cause of venture firm failure, and non-attribution, team-based investment models can reduce the internal politics that usually cause this.
repeat_guest_callback: if you haven't listened to Finn's prior episode, then that really is a must
notable: First-ever 20VC episode with two simultaneous guests, built around an exclusive announcement that Todd Jackson was joining First Round Capital full-time as a venture partner.
